1.lecture:
§1 · Systems and their boundaries

A system is whatever is under study; everything else is the surroundings, separated by a boundary. A closed system has a boundary that energy may cross, but mass may not — gas trapped in a sealed piston-cylinder is the standard example. An open system, or control volume, has a boundary crossed by both mass and energy — a pump, a length of pipe, a compressor. Almost every real piece of plant equipment is a control volume, which is why its own energy balance (Lesson 5) carries most of the weight for the rest of this course. An isolated system is the idealization where neither mass nor energy crosses at all — a useful limit case for the second law later in Lesson 8.

§2 · Properties: intensive versus extensive

An extensive property depends on how much of the system is present — mass, volume, and total internal energy all double if the system doubles. An intensive property does not — temperature, pressure, and density are the same everywhere in a uniform system regardless of how much of it is being examined. Dividing an extensive property by mass produces its specific (intensive) counterpart: specific volume \(v=V/m\), specific internal energy \(u=U/m\), specific enthalpy \(h=H/m\). This lower-case convention — capital for the total (extensive) quantity, lower-case for the per-unit-mass (intensive) quantity — is used without further comment for the rest of the course.

§3 · Equilibrium, process paths, and the state postulate

A system is in equilibrium when no unbalanced thermal, mechanical, or chemical potential remains to drive further change; only then does every property have one well-defined value throughout the system. A process is the change from one equilibrium state to another, and the specific sequence of states passed through along the way is its path — different paths can connect the same two endpoints, a fact Lesson 3 uses directly. The state postulate, for the simple compressible substances this course works with, says that any two independent intensive properties fix the state completely — every other property then follows. This single idea is why steam tables are organized the way they are: look up two known properties, read everything else.

§4 · Units and sign conventions — fixed once, forever

This course works in SI: kg, m, s, K for the base quantities; kJ for energy; kPa or bar for pressure; kJ/kg and kJ/(kg·K) for specific quantities. The sign convention, stated once so it never needs restating: heat transfer into the system is positive (\(+Q\)); work done by the system (output) is positive (\(+W\)) — the standard engineering convention. Under it, the first law (Lesson 4) reads \( \Delta U = Q - W \): heat in helps the balance, work out costs it. Inconsistent sign conventions across sources are a classic, avoidable source of error — this one is fixed here and used throughout.

Key rule — the state postulate

For a simple compressible substance, any TWO independent intensive properties fix the state completely.
1.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Basic mechanics units (kg, m, s, N) from Physics I.
  • The idea of a "state" from earlier courses' equilibrium concepts.
  • No prior thermodynamics — this lesson fixes vocabulary and conventions used for the rest of the course.

Toolkit — systems and properties

TermMeaningUse
Closed systemenergy crosses, mass does notPiston-cylinder problems (Lesson 4).
Open system / CVmass and energy both crossAlmost all real equipment (Lesson 5).
Extensive / intensivedepends on amount / does notSorts every property into one of two kinds.
Specific propertyextensive ÷ mass (v, u, h)The intensive form used in tables.

Sign convention+Q in, +W out\(\Delta U = Q - W\) (Lesson 4).

quiz.q:
Classifying three systems

Problem. Classify each as closed, open, or isolated: (a) a sealed pressure cooker before the valve opens; (b) a running centrifugal pump; (c) a perfectly insulated rigid tank, valve shut, nothing stirring.
quiz.solution: Solution. (a) Closed — heat may cross the sealed pot, but no mass does. (b) Open (control volume) — fluid flows continuously through it. (c) Isolated — with the valve shut and perfect insulation, neither mass nor energy crosses its boundary.

quiz.q:
Applying the sign convention

Problem. A gas in a piston-cylinder absorbs 40 kJ of heat and does 25 kJ of work pushing the piston outward. Using \(\Delta U = Q - W\), find the change in internal energy.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( \Delta U = 40 - 25 = 15\ \text{kJ} \) — the internal energy increases by 15 kJ; the gas kept more energy than it gave up as work.

quiz.q:
Which properties differ, which do not

Problem. Tank A holds 2 kg of water; Tank B holds 4 kg of the same water at the identical temperature and pressure. Which properties differ between the tanks, and which are identical?
quiz.solution: Solution. Extensive properties differ — total volume, total internal energy, and mass are roughly double in Tank B. Intensive properties are identical in both — temperature, pressure, specific volume, and specific internal energy do not depend on how much material is present.

quiz.q: A closed system's boundary may be crossed by:
quiz.solution: Closed means mass-tight; energy can still cross as heat or work.

quiz.q: An open system (control volume) boundary may be crossed by:
quiz.solution: This is the defining feature of a control volume, and the reason Lesson 5's balance is written the way it is.

quiz.q: An isolated system is one where:
choices: Neither mass nor energy crosses the boundary
quiz.solution: Isolated is the strictest case — a useful idealization for the second law.

quiz.q: Specific volume v = V/m is:
quiz.solution: Dividing an extensive property by mass produces its intensive, specific form.

quiz.q: Under the sign convention fixed in this lesson, heat transfer INTO the system is:
quiz.solution: +Q in is the convention this course uses throughout.

quiz.q: Under the same convention, work done BY the system (output) is:
quiz.solution: +W out pairs with +Q in to give ΔU = Q − W.

quiz.q: The state postulate for a simple compressible substance says the state is fixed by:
quiz.solution: Two independent intensive properties are enough — the basis of every steam-table lookup.

quiz.q: A property has a single, well-defined value throughout a system only when:
quiz.solution: Away from equilibrium, a property can vary from point to point within the same system.

1.kuwait:
A utilities engineer reading a plant P&ID applies §1 before touching any calculation: a boiler drum with the valves shut for maintenance is a closed system; the same drum in normal service, with feedwater in and steam out continuously, is an open system, and only the control-volume balance of Lesson 5 applies to it while running. Misclassifying which type of system is on the page is the single most common setup error in an energy-balance problem, well before any arithmetic goes wrong.

The sign convention of §4 is not a formality — a utilities log or a vendor datasheet that silently flips it (work-in positive instead of work-out positive, a real difference between some manufacturer conventions) produces energy balances that look plausible but are wrong by a sign, and a wrong-sign balance can make a losing piece of equipment look like it is performing normally. Fixing the convention once, in writing, before any numbers are entered is standard engineering discipline, not pedantry.

2.lecture:
§1 · The three regions

A pure substance's property chart has three regions. To the left of the dome: compressed (or subcooled) liquid — liquid below its saturation temperature at that pressure. Under the dome: the saturated mixture region, liquid and vapor coexisting at the saturation temperature for that pressure. To the right: superheated vapor — vapor above its saturation temperature, with no liquid present at all. The dome's peak is the critical point, where the distinction between liquid and vapor disappears entirely.

§2 · Quality — reading a point inside the dome

Quality \(x\) is the vapor mass fraction, \(x=m_{\text{vapor}}/m_{\text{total}}\), and it is defined only inside the dome — it is meaningless anywhere else on the chart. \(x=0\) is saturated liquid, the dome's left edge; \(x=1\) is saturated vapor, its right edge. \(x=0.85\) means \(85\)% of the mass is saturated vapor and the remaining \(15\)% is still saturated liquid, both sitting at the same saturation temperature and pressure. Any property \(y\) (specific volume, internal energy, enthalpy, entropy) at that quality is a quality-weighted average between its saturated-liquid and saturated-vapor values, \( y=y_f+x(y_g-y_f) \) — the same lever-rule idea used for a two-phase metal alloy, applied here to a boiling substance instead.

§3 · Superheat, subcooling, and why turbines want it

Superheat measures how far above the saturation temperature for its pressure the vapor actually sits; superheated vapor carries no liquid at all. Subcooling is the mirror case for a liquid below its saturation temperature. Turbines are supplied with superheated steam, not steam sitting inside the dome, because wet (two-phase) steam carries liquid droplets that strike the moving blades at high speed and erode them over years of service — keeping the steam superheated at inlet, and the expansion path through the turbine as dry as practical, protects the hardware.

§4 · Steam-table fluency — the working method

Given any two independent intensive properties (the state postulate, Lesson 1), the method is always the same three steps. (1) Identify which two properties are known. (2) Locate the region: compare the known values against the saturation values at that pressure or temperature — between \(v_f\) and \(v_g\) places the state inside the dome; below \(v_f\), compressed liquid; above \(v_g\), superheated. (3) Read or interpolate the remaining properties from the matching table — the saturation table with quality if inside the dome, the superheated table directly otherwise. This three-step method, not memorized numbers, is the actual skill a steam table drill is building.

Key tool — quality-weighted average

\[ y = y_f + x\,(y_g - y_f) \]
2.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • The state postulate (Lesson 1) — why two properties are always enough.
  • The lever rule (Materials Science II, Lesson 1) — the same weighted-average idea, different physics.
  • No prior exposure to steam tables assumed.

Toolkit — regions and quality

TermMeaning / formulaUse
Compressed liquidT below saturation T at that PLeft of the dome.
Saturated mixtureliquid + vapor coexistingInside the dome; needs quality x.
Superheated vaporT above saturation T at that PRight of the dome; no liquid present.
Quality x\( m_{vapor}/m_{total} \)0 = sat. liquid, 1 = sat. vapor.
Property at quality x\( y=y_f+x(y_g-y_f) \)Reads v, u, h, s inside the dome.

quiz.q:
What is physically in the vessel?

Problem. A rigid vessel contains a saturated liquid-vapor mixture of water at quality \(x=0.85\). What is physically inside the vessel, in plain terms?
quiz.solution: Solution. 85% of the mass inside is saturated vapor and 15% is still saturated liquid, both coexisting at the boiling temperature that corresponds to the vessel's pressure — not a homogeneous gas, and not all liquid.

quiz.q:
Reading specific volume at a given quality

Problem. At a certain pressure, saturated liquid water has \(v_f=0.001\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg}\) and saturated vapor has \(v_g=0.194\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg}\). Find the specific volume of a mixture at quality \(x=0.85\).
quiz.solution: Solution. \( v=v_f+x(v_g-v_f)=0.001+0.85(0.194-0.001)=0.001+0.85(0.193)\approx0.165\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg} \). Notice how close this is to the pure-vapor value — a small liquid fraction still occupies almost no volume compared to the vapor.

quiz.q:
Superheated or still wet?

Problem. Steam at a certain pressure has a saturation temperature of \(250^\circ\)C. It is measured at \(320^\circ\)C at that same pressure. Is it superheated or still inside the two-phase dome, and why does this matter for turbine blades?
quiz.solution: Solution. \(320^\circ\text{C} > 250^\circ\text{C}\), so the steam is superheated — no liquid droplets present. This matters because wet steam inside the dome carries liquid droplets that erode turbine blades on impact at high velocity; superheat keeps the entering steam liquid-free.

quiz.q: Quality x is defined:
choices: Only inside the two-phase (saturated mixture) dome
quiz.solution: Outside the dome, there is no liquid-vapor split for x to describe.

quiz.q: x = 0 corresponds to:
quiz.solution: x=0 is the dome's liquid edge.

quiz.q: x = 1 corresponds to:
quiz.solution: x=1 is the dome's vapor edge.

quiz.q: A property inside the dome at quality x is found by:
quiz.solution: The quality-weighted average between the saturated-liquid and saturated-vapor values.

quiz.q: Superheated vapor is:
choices: Above the saturation temperature for its pressure, with no liquid present
A mixture of liquid and vapor
quiz.solution: Superheat means clear of the dome entirely, on the vapor side.

quiz.q: Turbines are supplied with superheated (not wet) steam mainly to:
choices: Avoid liquid-droplet erosion of the blades
Increase the steam's quality to exactly 1.5
quiz.solution: Liquid droplets in wet steam strike blades at speed and erode them over time.

quiz.q: The critical point is where:
choices: The distinction between liquid and vapor disappears
quiz.solution: Above the critical point there is no boiling transition to speak of.

quiz.q: Given two independent intensive properties, the working method for a steam table starts by:
choices: Determining which region those two properties place the state in
Assuming the state is always superheated
quiz.solution: Region identification comes first; which table (and whether quality is needed) follows from it.

2.kuwait:
A utilities operator reading a boiler drum's pressure and a downstream temperature gauge is running the working method of §4 without necessarily naming it: comparing the measured temperature against the saturation temperature at that pressure is exactly how a shift engineer confirms the plant is actually delivering superheated steam to the turbine, not wet steam that will quietly erode blades over years of otherwise normal-looking operation.

Quality shows up constantly in condensate and flash-steam calculations: water throttled from a high-pressure boiler drum to a lower-pressure vessel partially flashes to steam, landing at some quality x between 0 and 1 rather than staying fully liquid — sizing a flash tank or a condensate return line without accounting for that quality-weighted specific volume is a common and avoidable undersizing mistake.

3.lecture:
§1 · Boundary work — path-dependent, and why

Boundary (moving-boundary) work is \( W=\int P\,dV \) for a quasi-equilibrium process — the area under the process curve on a P–v diagram. Because different paths between the same two states can trace different curves, and therefore enclose different areas, work is path-dependent: it is not a property fixed by the state alone, only by the specific route taken to get there. This is the direct contrast with internal energy, which is fixed entirely by the state and does not care how that state was reached. Standard processes give simple results: isobaric (constant \(P\)), \(W=P(V_2-V_1)\); isochoric (constant \(V\)), \(W=0\); isothermal ideal gas, \(W=mRT\ln(V_2/V_1)\); polytropic (\(PV^n=\text{const}\)), \(W=\dfrac{P_2V_2-P_1V_1}{1-n}\) for \(n\neq1\).

§2 · Heat — energy in transit, not a substance

Heat is energy crossing a boundary because of a temperature difference alone. A system does not "contain" heat the way it contains internal energy — heat, like work, is a path-dependent quantity describing how energy crosses the boundary, not a property of the state itself. An adiabatic process or boundary is one with \(Q=0\) — either perfectly insulated, or fast enough that meaningful heat transfer has no time to occur.

§3 · Reading work from the diagram — the working method

The area between a process curve and the volume axis, bounded by the initial and final volumes, is the boundary work for that process. A curve running higher in pressure encloses more area for the same volume change, meaning more work. A cycle — a closed loop on the P–v diagram, returning to its starting state — has net work equal to the area enclosed by the loop; this is the exact idea Lesson 11's engine cycles build on.

§4 · Working method for a work-and-heat problem

(1) Identify the process type — isobaric, isochoric, isothermal, polytropic, or a curve given directly — since that identification selects which formula or graphical area applies. (2) Compute or read off the boundary work. (3) If both work and an energy quantity are needed, combine with the first law (Lesson 4) to find whichever of heat or internal-energy change remains unknown. In a well-posed problem, work and heat are never both unknowns at once — one is always computable directly, and the first law supplies the other.

Key rule — boundary work

\[ W=\int P\,dV = \text{area under the process curve (P–v)}. \]
3.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • The state postulate and P–v/T–s style charts (Lessons 1–2).
  • Basic integration as "area under a curve" (Math I/II).
  • No prior first-law exposure — that arrives next lesson.

Toolkit — work and heat

ProcessBoundary workUse
Isobaric (const. P)\( W=P(V_2-V_1) \)Piston-cylinder at constant external P.
Isochoric (const. V)\( W=0 \)Rigid tank, any process.
Isothermal (ideal gas)\( W=mRT\ln(V_2/V_1) \)Slow, constant-T expansion/compression.
Polytropic (\(PV^n\)=const)\( W=\dfrac{P_2V_2-P_1V_1}{1-n} \)Real compressions/expansions, \(n\neq1\).
Adiabatic\(Q=0\)Insulated or very fast processes.

quiz.q:
Isobaric boundary work

Problem. A gas expands at a constant pressure of \(300\ \text{kPa}\) from \(V_1=0.02\ \text{m}^3\) to \(V_2=0.05\ \text{m}^3\). Find the boundary work.

Work in a rigid tank

Problem. A gas is heated in a rigid, sealed tank from \(20^\circ\)C to \(80^\circ\)C. What boundary work is done, and why?
quiz.solution: Solution. \(W=0\). The tank's boundary never moves, so no \(P\,dV\) work occurs regardless of how much the temperature or pressure changes inside.

quiz.q:
Two paths, same endpoints, different work

Problem. Two reversible paths connect \(P_1=400\ \text{kPa},\,V_1=0.01\ \text{m}^3\) to \(P_2=200\ \text{kPa},\,V_2=0.03\ \text{m}^3\). Path A: isobaric at \(400\ \text{kPa}\) (\(V\): \(0.01\to0.03\)), then isochoric (\(P\): \(400\to200\)). Path B: isochoric first (\(P\): \(400\to200\) at \(V=0.01\)), then isobaric at \(200\ \text{kPa}\) (\(V\): \(0.01\to0.03\)). Compute the work for each path.
quiz.solution: Solution. Path A: \(400\times(0.03-0.01)+0=8\ \text{kJ}\). Path B: \(0+200\times(0.03-0.01)=4\ \text{kJ}\). Same two endpoint states, different work — 8 kJ versus 4 kJ — confirming work depends on the path, not just the states.

quiz.q: Boundary work for a quasi-equilibrium process equals:
choices: The area under the process curve on a P–v diagram
The area under the T–s curve always
quiz.solution: W = ∫P dV is read directly as the area under the P–v curve.

quiz.q: Work is:
choices: A state property, like internal energy
quiz.solution: Unlike U, work depends on the specific path taken between two states.

quiz.q: For an isochoric (constant-volume) process, boundary work is:
choices: Zero, since the boundary never moves
quiz.solution: No volume change means no PdV work, whatever else changes.

quiz.q: For an isobaric process, boundary work equals:
quiz.solution: Constant pressure factors straight out of the integral.

quiz.q: Heat transfer is best described as:
choices: Energy in transit due to a temperature difference, not a substance a system contains
A property stored inside a system
Identical to work in every case
quiz.solution: A system has internal energy, not "heat content" — heat only exists while crossing a boundary.

quiz.q: An adiabatic process is one where:
quiz.solution: Adiabatic specifically means no heat transfer, whether by insulation or by speed.

quiz.q: A cycle's net work on a P–v diagram equals:
choices: The area enclosed by the loop
The area under the highest-pressure leg only
quiz.solution: Positive-work and negative-work legs net out to the enclosed loop area — the basis of Lesson 11's cycles.

quiz.q: Two different reversible paths between the same two states generally:
choices: Only differ in heat, never in work
quiz.solution: Different areas under different curves mean different work, even for identical endpoints.

3.kuwait:
A compressor's indicator diagram — pressure plotted against cylinder volume over one stroke — is a live P–v diagram, and the enclosed loop area a technician reads off it is literally the net work the compressor is doing per cycle. A worn valve or a leaking piston ring changes the shape of that loop, and an engineer trained to read the area rather than just a single pressure gauge reading catches the efficiency loss well before it shows up as a bigger utility bill.

Path-dependence is not just theory on a shutdown or startup sequence: bringing a pressure vessel up to operating pressure slowly (a long, gentle path) versus quickly (a short, steep path) can do noticeably different boundary work on any gas or vapor inside, even between the identical start and end pressures — a detail that matters when a startup procedure specifies rate limits, not just a final target.

4.lecture:
§1 · The energy balance, stated once

For a closed system, \( \Delta U = Q - W \) (using the sign convention fixed in Lesson 1). When kinetic and potential energy changes are negligible — true for almost every closed-system problem in this course — this is the entire first law needed. Every problem in this lesson is a variation on filling in this one equation.

§2 · Internal energy versus enthalpy — when each applies

Internal energy \(U\) is the natural variable at constant volume: a rigid tank does no boundary work (\(W=0\)), so \(Q=\Delta U\) directly — all the heat added raises internal energy. Enthalpy, \(H=U+PV\), is the natural variable at constant pressure: it can be shown that \(Q=\Delta H\) directly there, because the definition of \(H\) already absorbs the \(P\Delta V\) boundary-work term. This is exactly why enthalpy exists as a defined property — it is the energy accounting tool built for constant-pressure processes, the way \(U\) is built for constant-volume ones. For an ideal gas, both depend on temperature alone: \( \Delta U=mc_v\Delta T \), \( \Delta H=mc_p\Delta T \).

§3 · Rigid tank versus piston-cylinder, heated equally

Add the same heat \(Q\) to the same gas from the same starting temperature, in two different confinements. In a rigid tank, all of \(Q\) raises internal energy: \( \Delta T=Q/(mc_v) \). In a piston-cylinder held at constant pressure, part of \(Q\) does boundary work pushing the piston, leaving less to raise temperature: \( \Delta T=Q/(mc_p) \). Because \(c_p>c_v\) always (\(c_p=c_v+R\) for an ideal gas), the same heat produces a smaller temperature rise at constant pressure. For \(1\ \text{kg}\) of air at \(300\ \text{K}\) receiving \(50\ \text{kJ}\) (\(c_v=0.718\), \(c_p=1.005\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}\)): the rigid tank reaches \(369.6\ \text{K}\), the piston-cylinder reaches only \(349.8\ \text{K}\) — the rigid tank ends hotter, by exactly the factor \(c_p/c_v\approx1.4\).

§4 · Working method for a first-law closed-system problem

(1) Identify the confinement: rigid means constant volume, use \(U\) and \(c_v\); a piston free against a constant external pressure means constant pressure, use \(H\) and \(c_p\); anything else, use the general \( \Delta U=Q-W \) with the boundary work computed separately, as in Lesson 3. (2) Write the energy balance with the fixed sign convention. (3) Substitute the ideal-gas relation for \(\Delta U\) or \(\Delta H\). (4) Solve for whichever single quantity — \(Q\), \(W\), or the temperature change — is unknown.

Key rule — U vs. H

Constant \(V\): \(Q=\Delta U=mc_v\Delta T\). Constant \(P\): \(Q=\Delta H=mc_p\Delta T\). This is the reason enthalpy is a defined property.
4.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Boundary work (Lesson 3) — what the constant-pressure case diverts heat into.
  • The sign convention (Lesson 1).
  • Ideal-gas relations from Physics I / Math background.

Toolkit — the first law, closed systems

TermFormulaUse
First law (closed)\( \Delta U = Q-W \)General closed-system balance.
Constant volume\( Q=\Delta U=mc_v\Delta T \)Rigid tank, sealed.
Constant pressure\( Q=\Delta H=mc_p\Delta T \)Free piston, constant external P.
\(c_p\) vs. \(c_v\)\( c_p=c_v+R \) (ideal gas)Explains why constant-P heating is "less efficient" at raising T.

quiz.q:
Rigid tank vs. piston-cylinder, real numbers

Problem. 1 kg of air at 300 K receives 50 kJ of heat: (a) in a rigid, sealed tank; (b) in a piston-cylinder at constant pressure. Using \(c_v=0.718\), \(c_p=1.005\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}\), find the final temperature in each case and say which ends hotter.
quiz.solution: Solution. Rigid: \( \Delta T=50/0.718\approx69.6\ \text{K}\to T_2\approx369.6\ \text{K}\). Piston: \( \Delta T=50/1.005\approx49.8\ \text{K}\to T_2\approx349.8\ \text{K}\). The rigid tank ends hotter — none of its heat is diverted into boundary work.

quiz.q:
Choosing U or H

Problem. A piston-cylinder holds air at constant pressure while \(30\ \text{kJ/kg}\) of heat is added. Which specific-heat relation applies directly, and why is internal energy alone not the right tool?
quiz.solution: Solution. \(c_p\) applies directly: \(q=\Delta h=c_p\Delta T\). Internal energy alone is not enough because, at constant pressure, part of the added heat does boundary work rather than raising U directly — enthalpy is defined precisely to absorb that boundary-work term.

quiz.q:
Same heat, different confinement — reasoning without numbers

Problem. A rigid tank of air rises 40 K for a given heat input. If the same heat input were applied instead at constant pressure to the same mass, would the resulting temperature rise be larger, smaller, or the same? Explain.
quiz.solution: Solution. Smaller. Since \(c_p>c_v\), the same heat input produces a smaller \(\Delta T\) at constant pressure — part of the heat is diverted into boundary work pushing the piston, rather than entirely raising internal energy as it does in the rigid tank.

quiz.q: At constant volume, boundary work is zero, so:
quiz.solution: With W=0, the first law collapses to Q=ΔU.

quiz.q: At constant pressure, the boundary-work term is absorbed by defining:
quiz.solution: H is built specifically so Q=ΔH works at constant pressure.

quiz.q: For an ideal gas, internal energy depends on:
choices: Pressure and volume, but not temperature
quiz.solution: U=U(T) only, for an ideal gas — the basis of ΔU=mcvΔT.

quiz.q: cp is always:
choices: Greater than cv, by the amount R
Equal to cv for every gas
quiz.solution: cp = cv + R for an ideal gas — cp is always the larger of the two.

quiz.q: Given the same heat input, a rigid tank versus a piston-cylinder at constant pressure:
choices: The rigid tank reaches a higher final temperature
The piston-cylinder reaches a higher final temperature
Both reach exactly the same temperature
quiz.solution: No heat is diverted to boundary work in the rigid tank, so it ends hotter.

quiz.q: ΔH = m·cp·ΔT applies directly to:
quiz.solution: The constant-pressure case is exactly what enthalpy was defined for.

quiz.q: The physical reason cp is greater than cv is:
choices: At constant pressure, some heat must also do boundary work, not just raise temperature
Pressure always adds energy for free
Volume has no effect on heat capacity
quiz.solution: The "extra" heat capacity in cp is exactly the boundary-work share.

quiz.q: The general closed-system energy balance, under this course's sign convention, is:
quiz.solution: +Q in, +W out, matching Lesson 1's fixed convention.

4.kuwait:
A relief-valve or overpressure study on a sealed, rigid vessel exposed to fire is a direct §3 application: with no way for the gas inside to expand and do work, every bit of absorbed heat drives temperature (and therefore pressure) up faster than the same heat would in an equivalent vessel with a free-moving boundary — a real reason rigid, fully-enclosed vessels are treated as a more severe fire case in relief-system sizing than a vented or piston-type system.

Choosing whether to model a heating or cooling step as constant-volume or constant-pressure is a routine first step before any plant energy balance is run, and getting it backward — using cv where cp applies, or the reverse — produces a temperature or heat-duty estimate that is wrong by roughly the cp/cv ratio, about 40% for air, which is large enough to matter in an equipment sizing calculation.

5.lecture:
§1 · The steady-flow energy equation, term by term

For a steady, single-inlet/single-outlet control volume, per unit mass: \[ q - w = (h_2-h_1) + \frac{V_2^2-V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2-z_1). \] Heat and work (\(q\), \(w\)) cross the boundary the same way they do in a closed system. Enthalpy \(h\), not internal energy alone, is the natural variable here because it automatically carries the flow work that mass brings with it as it crosses the boundary — extending the constant-pressure insight from Lesson 4 to a continuously flowing stream. \(V\) is velocity (the kinetic-energy term) and \(z\) is elevation (the potential-energy term). "Steady" means nothing inside the control volume changes with time — the same mass flow rate in as out, the same properties at every point, moment to moment; steady does not mean nothing is happening, only that nothing is changing.

§2 · The five one-line devices

A nozzle or diffuser does no work and is usually adiabatic; it trades enthalpy for kinetic energy (nozzle) or the reverse (diffuser): \( \Delta h\approx-\Delta KE \). A turbine produces work, is usually adiabatic, and its KE/PE changes are typically negligible next to its enthalpy drop, so \( w\approx h_1-h_2 \) — the enthalpy drop across a real turbine is commonly hundreds of kJ/kg, while velocity changes contribute only a few kJ/kg, which is why KE is routinely dropped without changing the answer meaningfully. A compressor or pump consumes work with the same simplifications, energy flowing the other way: \( w\approx h_2-h_1 \). A throttle does no work, transfers negligible heat, and has negligible KE/PE change, leaving \(h_1=h_2\) exactly — an isenthalpic device. A heat exchanger does no work, has negligible KE/PE change, and reduces to \( q=h_2-h_1 \) for each stream.

§3 · Reading a device — the working method

For any of these five: (1) start from the full SFEE; (2) cross out whatever the device's physical description rules out — no work means \(w=0\); adiabatic means \(q=0\); a large flow area and low speed mean \( \Delta KE\) is negligible; no elevation change means \( \Delta PE=0\); (3) whatever survives is the device's governing equation. This is not five separate formulas to memorize — it is one equation, specialized five different ways by exactly the same logic each time.

§4 · Why this equation dominates the next four years

Pumps, compressors, turbines, heat exchangers, control valves, HVAC coils — nearly every piece of process, rotating, or thermal equipment a mechanical engineer touches reduces to some simplified form of this one balance. Fast, confident simplification, the three steps of §3, is the single most repeatedly used skill this course builds, and it is used weekly for the rest of a working career in this field.

Key result — the throttle

No work, negligible heat, negligible ΔKE/ΔPE ⇒ \( h_1=h_2 \). The sharpest example of "cross out what does not apply."
5.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Enthalpy (Lesson 4) — why it, not u, is the flow-energy variable.
  • Open systems / control volumes (Lesson 1).
  • Steady-state as "unchanging in time," not "nothing happening."

Toolkit — the SFEE and its five devices

DeviceGoverning formUse
Nozzle / diffuser\( \Delta h\approx-\Delta KE \)No work; converts h ↔ KE.
Turbine\( w\approx h_1-h_2 \)Work out; KE/PE usually negligible.
Compressor / pump\( w\approx h_2-h_1 \)Work in.
Throttle\( h_1=h_2 \)Isenthalpic; no work, no significant heat.
Heat exchanger\( q=h_2-h_1 \) per streamNo work; KE/PE negligible.

quiz.q:
The throttle, from the full SFEE

Problem. Write the SFEE for a throttle (valve) and state which terms survive and which vanish.
quiz.solution: Solution. No work (\(w=0\)), no significant heat (\(q\approx0\)), negligible \(\Delta KE\) and \(\Delta PE\) — the SFEE collapses to \(0=h_2-h_1\), i.e. \(h_1=h_2\). A throttle is isenthalpic.

quiz.q:
How small is ΔKE, really?

Problem. A steam turbine has an enthalpy drop of \(600\ \text{kJ/kg}\). Inlet velocity is \(60\ \text{m/s}\), exit velocity is \(120\ \text{m/s}\). Compute the kinetic-energy change per unit mass and compare it to the enthalpy drop.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( \Delta KE=\dfrac{V_2^2-V_1^2}{2}=\dfrac{120^2-60^2}{2}=\dfrac{10800}{2}=5400\ \text{J/kg}=5.4\ \text{kJ/kg} \). Against \( \Delta h=600\ \text{kJ/kg}\), that is under 1% — confirming ΔKE is routinely negligible next to a turbine enthalpy drop.

quiz.q:
Balancing a two-stream heat exchanger

Problem. In a two-stream heat exchanger with no heat loss to the surroundings, the hot stream enthalpy drops by \(80\ \text{kJ/kg}\) at \(2\ \text{kg/s}\). The cold stream flows at \(4\ \text{kg/s}\). What enthalpy rise does the cold stream receive?
quiz.solution: Solution. Energy given up equals energy received: \( \dot m_{hot}\Delta h_{hot}=\dot m_{cold}\Delta h_{cold} \Rightarrow 2\times80=4\times\Delta h_{cold} \Rightarrow \Delta h_{cold}=40\ \text{kJ/kg} \).

quiz.q: A throttle is best described as:
choices: Isenthalpic — no work, negligible heat, h1 = h2
A device that always raises enthalpy
A device with large KE change
quiz.solution: Everything cancels except the enthalpy balance itself.

quiz.q: A turbine's work output is well approximated by:
quiz.solution: Enthalpy drops as the turbine extracts work; KE/PE are usually negligible beside it.

quiz.q: A compressor's work input is well approximated by:
quiz.solution: Work in raises enthalpy — the opposite sign convention from a turbine.

quiz.q: A nozzle converts:
quiz.solution: A diffuser does the reverse conversion.

quiz.q: "Steady" flow means:
choices: Properties at each point in the control volume do not change with time
Nothing is happening inside the device
Mass flow rate is always zero
Only heat can cross the boundary
quiz.solution: Plenty is happening — it just is not changing moment to moment.

quiz.q: Enthalpy, not internal energy alone, is the natural variable for control volumes because:
choices: It automatically includes the flow work carried by mass crossing the boundary
Internal energy does not exist for flowing streams
Enthalpy is always larger than internal energy
It removes the need for a sign convention
quiz.solution: The PV term flowing mass carries is exactly what enthalpy adds to internal energy.

quiz.q: The working method for any SFEE device is:
choices: Start from the full equation and cross out terms the device's description rules out
Always assume all terms are significant
quiz.solution: One equation, five specializations — not five separate formulas.

quiz.q: A heat exchanger (no work, negligible KE/PE) reduces the SFEE to:
choices: q = h2 − h1 for each stream
quiz.solution: Heat crosses the boundary of each stream separately, with no shaft work involved.

5.kuwait:
A control-room operator watching a steam turbine's inlet and outlet pressure gauges is implicitly relying on §2's approximation every shift: the turbine's power output is read almost entirely from the enthalpy drop across it, with velocity and elevation changes assumed away — which is exactly why a sudden, unexplained drop in output at the same steam conditions is read as an internal problem (blade wear, seal leakage) rather than a change in some KE or PE term nobody is tracking, because those terms were never significant to begin with.

Pressure-reducing stations on a steam or gas distribution line are throttles in the strict sense of §2, and the isenthalpic result explains a genuinely counter-intuitive field observation: throttled steam can come out measurably colder (or, for some gases, warmer) than it went in, at the same enthalpy, purely from the pressure drop — a fact worth knowing before assuming a temperature change downstream of a valve means something is leaking or malfunctioning.

6.lecture:
§1 · Why steady-flow breaks down

The SFEE of Lesson 5 assumes nothing inside the control volume changes with time. Charging or discharging a tank violates that directly: the mass and stored energy inside change continuously as it fills or empties. The standard fix is the uniform-state, uniform-flow (USUF) model — the properties of fluid crossing the boundary are taken as uniform throughout the process, and the state inside the control volume is uniform at any given instant, even though it changes from instant to instant. This keeps the mass and energy balances tractable while still capturing the transient honestly.

§2 · The transient energy balance

Mass balance: \( m_2-m_1=m_{in}-m_{out} \) — the final mass inside minus the initial mass equals the net mass that crossed the boundary. Energy balance: \( m_2u_2-m_1u_1=Q-W+m_{in}h_{in}-m_{out}h_{out} \). Mass flowing in or out carries enthalpy — it is moving, so it carries flow work exactly as in Lesson 5 — while mass already inside, or accumulating inside, is accounted with internal energy, since it is not flowing anywhere and so carries no flow-work term.

§3 · Charging an evacuated, insulated tank

The sharpest special case: an evacuated tank (\(m_1=0\)), perfectly insulated (\(Q=0\)), rigid (\(W=0\)), charged from a supply line at constant state \(h_{in}\). The balances reduce to \(m_2=m_{in}\) and \( m_2u_2=m_{in}h_{in} \), so simply \( u_2=h_{in} \). For an ideal gas, \(u=c_vT\) and \(h=c_pT\), giving \( c_vT_2=c_pT_{in}\Rightarrow T_2=(c_p/c_v)T_{in}=kT_{in} \). For air (\(k\approx1.4\)) charged from a \(300\ \text{K}\) (\(27^\circ\)C) line, the tank ends at \(420\ \text{K}\) (\(147^\circ\)C) — nearly \(120^\circ\)C hotter than the line it was filled from, with no heater anywhere in the picture. The entering gas carries flow work in addition to its internal energy; once inside the tank, that flow work has nowhere to go except into raising the internal energy — the temperature — of the gas now sitting still. This is a real, measurable effect: rapid receiver filling genuinely runs hotter than the supply, occasionally hot enough to matter for downstream components rated to the supply-line temperature alone.

§4 · Where steady-flow honestly fails — the working method

Ask: does anything inside the control volume's boundary change measurably over the timescale being studied? A pump or turbine running continuously at a fixed load: no — steady-flow is safe. A tank filling or draining, a startup or shutdown, a batch step: yes — the transient (USUF) balance is required instead. A slow transient can often be treated as a sequence of steady states ("quasi-steady"), but a genuinely fast charge or discharge, like this lesson's tank, needs the full transient treatment to avoid a real, sometimes large, error.

Key result — charging an evacuated tank

\[ T_2 = \frac{c_p}{c_v}\,T_{line} = k\,T_{line}\qquad(\text{insulated, rigid, initially evacuated}). \]
6.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • The SFEE and why mass carries enthalpy (Lesson 5).
  • cp, cv, and k=cp/cv (Lesson 4).
  • Mass and energy balances generally, from earlier lessons.

Toolkit — transient flow

TermFormulaUse
USUF modeluniform state, uniform flowStandard transient-flow idealization.
Mass balance\( m_2-m_1=m_{in}-m_{out} \)Tracks accumulated mass.
Energy balance\( m_2u_2-m_1u_1=Q-W+m_{in}h_{in}-m_{out}h_{out} \)In/out streams use h; contents use u.
Evacuated-tank charging\( T_2=(c_p/c_v)T_{in} \)Tank ends hotter than the supply line.

quiz.q:
Charging temperature, computed

Problem. An evacuated, well-insulated rigid tank is opened to an air supply line at 300 K until pressures equalize, then shut. Using \(k=c_p/c_v=1.4\) for air, find the final tank temperature and explain in one sentence why it exceeds the supply line temperature.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( T_2=1.4\times300=420\ \text{K}\ (147^\circ\text{C}) \), nearly 120°C above the 300 K (27°C) line — because the entering gas carries flow work in addition to its internal energy, and that flow work has nowhere to go but into raising the internal energy of the now-stationary gas inside the tank.

quiz.q:
A direct mass balance

Problem. A 0.5 m³ tank initially holds 0.2 kg of a gas. After a valve is opened and gas flows in, the tank ends up holding 0.9 kg. How much mass entered through the valve?

Steady or transient?

Problem. State whether the steady-flow assumption is appropriate for: (a) a compressor running continuously at a fixed load; (b) a compressed-air receiver being filled from empty over 30 seconds. Justify each.
quiz.solution: Solution. (a) Steady-flow is appropriate — nothing inside the compressor's control volume changes with time once it runs at a fixed load. (b) Steady-flow is not appropriate — the receiver's mass and internal energy change continuously as it fills, exactly the transient (USUF) case this lesson develops.

quiz.q: The uniform-state, uniform-flow model is used when:
choices: Mass and energy inside the control volume change with time
quiz.solution: USUF is the standard fix for exactly the case steady-flow cannot handle.

quiz.q: In the transient energy balance, mass flowing in or out is accounted with:
quiz.solution: Flowing mass carries flow work, so enthalpy is the correct accounting variable.

quiz.q: Mass already inside (or accumulating inside) the control volume is accounted with:
quiz.solution: Stationary mass carries no flow-work term, so u is the correct variable for it.

quiz.q: For an evacuated, insulated, rigid tank charged from a line at constant state, the balances reduce to:
quiz.solution: With m1=0, Q=0, W=0, the energy balance simplifies to exactly this.

quiz.q: Charging such a tank from a 300 K air supply (k=1.4) leaves the tank at approximately:
The tank ends up hotter than the supply line because:
choices: The entering flow work has nowhere to go but into raising the internal energy of the gas now inside
Compressing the incoming gas releases chemical energy
The supply line is actually hotter than measured
quiz.solution: No external heater is involved — the effect is purely from flow work converting to internal energy.

quiz.q: Steady-flow is a safe assumption when:
choices: Nothing measurable inside the control volume changes over the timescale of interest
The fluid is a liquid, never a gas
The process lasts under one second
quiz.solution: The test is whether internal conditions change over the timescale being studied, not the device's size or fluid type.

quiz.q: A tank filling from empty over a short time is best modeled with:
choices: The transient (USUF) balance, not the steady-flow energy equation
quiz.solution: Changing internal mass and energy is exactly what steady-flow cannot represent.

6.kuwait:
Compressed-air receivers filling quickly after a compressor starts, or after a large downstream demand empties and then refills a header, genuinely run measurably hotter than the compressor's discharge line temperature alone would suggest — an instrumentation or safety review that only checks the compressor's rated discharge temperature, without accounting for this transient charging effect on a receiver downstream, can under-rate what a fitting or gauge near that receiver actually experiences during a fast refill.

Nitrogen or inert-gas purge systems, filled quickly into an evacuated or near-empty vessel before a maintenance entry, show the identical effect: the vessel can read noticeably warmer than the supply cylinder immediately after a fast fill, a detail worth knowing before assuming a warm gauge means a leak or a fault rather than simple, expected thermodynamics.
